content:
Case 2:
Initial presentation:
- Age: 24
- Sex: female
- Chief complaint: New skin changes on the back and extremities
- Vital signs: BP 120/75 mmHg, HR 116/min, T° 36.8 °C, respiratory rate 16/min.
Setting: Outpatient/Day Unit (Initial Presentation)
Capabilities: You are evaluating a clinically stable patient with new symptoms in an ambulatory or day-unit setting. You can perform a focused history and examination, obtain routine blood panels, and arrange standard non-invasive imaging as first-line tests. Invasive tissue sampling and specialized analyses usually require additional coordination and are not available in this round.
Provide either a request for a single information item or solve.

content:
History Physical:
On June 4, 2025, the patient presented with new skin changes, first noticed by her partner on the back and now also on the extremities. She reported intermittent headaches and mild nausea, as well as chronic joint pain and stiffness. Otherwise, she was in good general condition, afebrile, and showed no signs of skin or mucosal GvHD. A marked psychological stress reaction was noted.
Current medication: pantoprazole 40 mg 1-0-0, vitamin D 20,000 IE 1×/week, calcium 500 mg 1-0-0, posaconazole 300 mg 1×/d, ruxolitinib 10 mg 1-0-1, valaciclovir 500 mg 1-0-1, co-trimoxazole 960 mg orally once daily, three times per week (Monday, Wednesday, Friday), steroid cream p.r.n.

solve.working_diagnosis: Suspected post–allogeneic transplant lymphoproliferative disorder (PTLD)/relapsed lymphoma with extranodal masses (cardiac pericardial/septal infiltrative lesion and retroperitoneal peri-ureteral lesions causing hydronephrosis), presenting with secondary skin changes
differentials: Extramedullary relapse of underlying hematologic malignancy (e.g., AML/ALL) with cardiac and retroperitoneal chloromas
Systemic vasculitis/connective-tissue disease with cardiac involvement (e.g., lupus/ANCA vasculitis)
Invasive fungal infection with cardiac/retroperitoneal lesions (despite posaconazole)

treatment_plan.action: Arrange urgent cardiac MRI and whole-body PET-CT for staging and characterization of cardiac/peri-ureteral lesions
Obtain tissue diagnosis: image-guided biopsy of the retroperitoneal peri-ureteral lesion (preferred) with histology, immunophenotyping/flow cytometry, and EBER/EBV testing
Coordinate urology for decompression of hydronephrosis (ureteral stent or nephrostomy) if worsening renal function/pain/infection; monitor creatinine closely
Check plasma EBV PCR and reduce immunosuppression as feasible (review ruxolitinib dosing) while awaiting diagnostics, balancing GVHD risk

If PTLD confirmed: initiate rituximab-based therapy (rituximab ± chemo depending on monomorphic subtype) and consider adoptive EBV-specific T-cell therapy in refractory disease; involve transplant/hematology team

Skin Biopsy:
Skin biopsy (2025-06-07): The dermis and subcutis are diffusely infiltrated by atypical hematopoietic cells in a perivascular and interstitial distribution. The cells show irregular nuclear contours, vesicular chromatin, and occasional nucleoli. Mitotic figures are frequent. Immunohistochemistry: CD34-, CD33+, MPO+, Lysozyme and CD68+, CD117+, negative for CD20-, CD19-, CD3-. Molecular pathology: NPM1 mutation present in cutaneous infiltration.

solve.working_diagnosis: Extramedullary relapse of NPM1-mutated acute myeloid leukemia (AML) with leukemia cutis and suspected concurrent cardiac/pericardial and peri-ureteral myeloid sarcoma (chloroma) after prior allogeneic transplant (on ruxolitinib)
differentials: Post-transplant lymphoproliferative disorder (EBV-driven, CD20+ B-cell process) with cutaneous and extranodal disease
Invasive fungal infection with FDG-avid lesions and cardiac/pericardial involvement
Cutaneous vasculitis/connective-tissue disease with secondary inflammatory FDG uptake (less likely given MPO+/CD33+ infiltrate and NPM1 mutation)

treatment_plan.action: Perform bone marrow biopsy with cytogenetics/NGS and measurable residual disease assessment to determine medullary involvement and guide salvage therapy

Start AML relapse management planning urgently: initiate salvage AML-directed therapy (choice per prior therapies and fitness; consider FLAG-IDA/CLAG-M or venetoclax-based regimen) and evaluate for second allogeneic transplant or donor lymphocyte infusion once disease controlled

Obtain targeted biopsy of an accessible extracutaneous FDG-avid lesion (peri-ureteral mass if feasible) to confirm myeloid sarcoma and exclude mixed/alternative pathology; correlate with NPM1 mutation

Manage organ-threatened sites: involve cardiology/cardio-oncology for monitoring and consideration of steroids/radiation only if hemodynamic compromise; involve urology for relief of hydronephrosis (stent/nephrostomy) if obstructive renal injury or infection develops

Continue infection prophylaxis and institute tumor lysis monitoring/prophylaxis (hydration, allopurinol/rasburicase as indicated) during cytoreductive therapy; review immunosuppression (ruxolitinib) with transplant team balancing GVHD vs relapse control

content:
Treatment History:
Diagnosis: 2024-02-21, AML with NPM1 mutation (WHO 2022). ELN 2022 risk: favorable.
Initial immunophenotype (February 21, 2024): CD34^low^ blasts expressing CD33, CD14, CD64, CD117, MPO, and CD13, negative for HLA-DR, CD7, CD56, and TdT.
Induction (Cycle 1): “7+3” (cytarabine 200 mg/m² for 7 days (2,352 mg total) + daunorubicin 60 mg/m² for 3 days (303 mg total)) plus gemtuzumab ozogamicin 5 mg on days 1, 4, and 7 (15 mg total). Complications: Neutropenic fever and pneumonia, otherwise well tolerated.
Consolidation (March–July 2024): Four cycles of high-dose cytarabine (3 g/m² BID for 3 days = 30,240 mg total per cycle). Response: Complete remission (CR). MRD negativity in bone marrow (flow cytometry and NPM1 PCR) achieved after the first consolidation cycle.
September 14, 2024: Patient noted palpable swellings in the chest. Gynecologic evaluation showed bead-like lymph nodes on ultrasound. Biopsy confirmed AML infiltration. Repeated bone marrow biopsy demonstrated ongoing CR but increased NPM1 levels (ratio 1.42). PET-CT was not performed. Donor search initiated.
Salvage therapy (September 20, 2024): Venetoclax 400 mg days 1–28 plus azacitidine 75 mg/m² days 1–7. September 28, 2024: Hospitalization due to neutropenic fever. Meropenem 1 g TID was initiated for 7 days (21 g total). Posaconazole 300 mg/day was started on day 13 of cycle 1, and venetoclax was reduced to 70 mg for the last 14 days (total dose 6,180 mg). Patient was discharged on October 8, 2024. After one cycle, extramedullary lesions were no longer palpable. Neutrophils recovered on October 18, 2024. PET-CT confirmed no further delineable lesions.
Second cycle (November 2, 2024): Venetoclax 70 mg days 1–14 (due to comedication with posaconazole 300 mg), achieving complete remission.
Allogeneic SCT (December 7, 2024): Haploidentical sibling donor; conditioning with fludarabine 30 mg/m² for 5 days, treosulfan 14 g/m² for 3 days, and thiotepa 5 mg/kg twice on day -2. GVHD prophylaxis: Post-transplant cyclophosphamide (50 mg/kg on days +3 and +4), mycophenolate mofetil (MMF), and tacrolimus.
Complications: Febrile episodes with suspected central venous catheter infection. Empiric antibiotic therapy was initiated with piperacillin/tazobactam 4.5 g TID for 3 days, later escalated to meropenem 1 g i.v. TID for 13 days, and vancomycin 1 g BID (later increased to 1.5 g-0-1.25 g for 5 days) for Gram-positive coverage. Hemolysis was suspected (haptoglobin <10 mg/dL, schistocytes 6‰ on peripheral smear, and LDH 324 U/L, bilirubin 1.2 mg/dL) in the setting of ABO-incompatible transplantation. The patient received rituximab 375 mg/m² i.v. weekly × 4 doses (total 2,520 mg).
January 10, 2025: Complete remission (CR); 100% donor chimerism; no MRD detectable in bone marrow.
March 28, 2025 (day +100): PET-CT showed no FDG-avid lesions. Day +107: Patient developed skin GvHD grade III°, steroid-refractory (Days 1–7: prednisolone 120 mg daily, tapered by 10 mg every 3 days) but responsive to ruxolitinib 10 mg BID. GvHD overlapped into mild to moderate chronic GvHD, controlled with ruxolitinib 10 mg BID.

consider second allo-HSCT if inadequate response or short remission, especially with high-burden/extramedullary disease", "intention": "therapeutic", "priority": 2}, {"action": "Local/organ-directed management of bulky or symptomatic sites: involve cardio-oncology; consider involved-site radiotherapy to cardiac/pericardial and/or peri-ureteral mass if organ compromise or inadequate systemic response; involve urology for ureteral stent/nephrostomy if renal function worsens or obstruction is clinically significant", "intention": "supportive", "priority": 2}, {"action": "Adjust immunosuppression with transplant team to enhance graft-versus-leukemia effect (stepwise reduction of ruxolitinib/tacrolimus if still used), balancing risk of recurrent severe GVHD; continue antimicrobial prophylaxis and institute tumor lysis monitoring/prophylaxis and transfusion support during cytoreduction", "intention": "supportive", "priority": 3}]}}}

solve.working_diagnosis: Post–allogeneic HSCT extramedullary relapse of NPM1-mutated acute myeloid leukemia (AML) presenting as leukemia cutis with PET-avid probable myeloid sarcoma involvement of pericardium/septum and retroperitoneal peri-ureteral mass causing hydronephrosis, with no marrow status yet documented in this round
differentials: Isolated extramedullary myeloid sarcoma without marrow relapse (vs combined marrow relapse)
Post-transplant lymphoproliferative disorder (unlikely given MPO+/CD33+/NPM1+ and CD20−)
Infectious/inflammatory mimics causing FDG uptake (very unlikely with diagnostic skin biopsy)

treatment_plan.action: Restage urgently with bone marrow aspirate/biopsy including morphology, flow, cytogenetics, and NPM1 RT-qPCR MRD; assess donor chimerism to determine isolated EM relapse vs systemic relapse

Initiate relapse-directed AML therapy promptly (clinical trial preferred): consider venetoclax-based salvage (e.g., venetoclax + hypomethylating agent or venetoclax + low-dose cytarabine) or intensive salvage (FLAG-IDA/CLAG-M) depending on fitness, prior toxicity, and treating center strategy, with intent to achieve remission prior to cellular therapy

Coordinate cellular therapy strategy: if remission achieved, plan donor lymphocyte infusion (DLI) given 100% donor chimerism history, with careful GVHD risk management; consider second allo-HSCT if inadequate response or short remission, especially with high-burden/extramedullary disease

Local/organ-directed management of bulky or symptomatic sites: involve cardio-oncology; consider involved-site radiotherapy to cardiac/pericardial and/or peri-ureteral mass if organ compromise or inadequate systemic response; involve urology for ureteral stent/nephrostomy if renal function worsens or obstruction is clinically significant

Adjust immunosuppression with transplant team to enhance graft-versus-leukemia effect (stepwise reduction of ruxolitinib/tacrolimus if still used), balancing risk of recurrent severe GVHD; continue antimicrobial prophylaxis and institute tumor lysis monitoring/prophylaxis and transfusion support during cytoreduction
